category_signature.one_sentence_summary: Position this category as a player-coach AI engineering leader who ships and scales AI platform or product capabilities, translates between stakeholders and engineering, and proves quality, governance, and adoption without overstating formal product-management or org-ownership claims.
distinctive_signals: The real archetype is delivery-led manager or player-coach, not executive: manager scope appears in 14/31 roles (45.2%), IC scope in 11/31 (35.5%), player-coach in 5/31 (16.1%), and executive in 1/31 (3.2%).

Architecture and productionization matter more than pure ML depth: platform design appears in 17/31 roles (54.8%), architecture is a valued evidence type in 11/17 deep analyses, and greenfield-or-both work appears 23 times versus optimization-only 1.

This category is differentiated by shipped AI value plus trust signals, not advanced experimentation or pure PM branding: evaluation/quality appears in 7/31 (22.6%), guardrails/governance in 6/31 (19.4%), GDPR in 10/31 (32.3%), while advanced experimentation-heavy and advanced academic requirements are both 0/31 (0.0%).
citations: Role scope distribution: manager 14/31 (45.2%), IC 11/31 (35.5%), player-coach 5/31 (16.1%), executive 1/31 (3.2%).
Architecture signals: platform design 17/31 (54.8%); architecture valued evidence type 11/17 deep analyses.
Trust signals: evaluation/quality 7/31 (22.6%), guardrails/governance 6/31 (19.4%), GDPR 10/31 (32.3%).
Disqualifiers: requires_phd 0/31 (0.0%), research_heavy 0/31 (0.0%).

headline_pattern.recommended_structure: [Safe engineering-manager family title] + [AI platform / GenAI delivery scope] + [architecture/evaluation/governance proof] + [measurable adoption or scale outcome]
evidence_first_rules: Lead the headline with shipped AI platform or GenAI delivery language because delivery and scale are valued evidence types in 17/17 deep analyses each.
Name architecture or platform design explicitly if true because platform design appears in 17/31 roles (54.8%) and architecture appears in 11/17 deep analyses.
Add one trust signal such as evaluation, guardrails, or GDPR only if true because evaluation/quality appears in 7/31 (22.6%), guardrails/governance in 6/31 (19.4%), and GDPR in 10/31 (32.3%).
Attach one measurable outcome in the headline or profile because measurable value, quality, delivery, and user impact each appear in 10/17 deep analyses.
citations: Title families are noisy: engineering_manager 8/31 vs other 23/31; top literal title variant is Product Manager, AI at 13/31, so headline safety should follow role-scope evidence, not raw title frequency.
Delivery and scale are each valued evidence types in 17/17 deep analyses.
Success metrics: measurable value 10/17, quality 10/17, delivery 10/17, user impact 10/17.

positioning_angle: Engineering leader who shipped AI platform or workflow capabilities end to end and can translate business priorities into reliable technical delivery.
Player-coach manager who combines platform architecture, evaluation, governance, and stakeholder alignment to drive adoption and measurable value.
foreground: Show delivery, scale, architecture, evaluation, governance, adoption, and stakeholder translation; communication appears in 30/31 (96.8%), collaboration in 27/31 (87.1%), stakeholder management in 21/31 (67.7%), while delivery, scale, and evaluation lead the deep-analysis evidence mix at 17/17, 17/17, and 15/17.
avoid: Avoid presenting as a formal product owner, GTM owner, pricing owner, or product-marketing lead unless directly evidenced; unsafe-claim signals on PM ownership, GTM, pricing, and product marketing each appear repeatedly across 2-3/17 deep analyses.

Avoid making people-management or budget claims unless explicit; hiring and performance management each appear in only 8/31 roles (25.8%), org-building in 12/31 (38.7%), and budget/P&L in 2/31 (6.5%).
safe_positioning: Safe positioning is engineering-led AI delivery with cross-functional roadmap translation, platform design, evaluation, and adoption proof; this aligns to pain points around AI roadmaps (6 mentions), prototyping/testing/deploying AI capabilities (3), alignment across engineering/design/leadership/executives (3), and end-to-end outcomes (3).
unsafe_positioning: Unsafe positioning is GM-style product ownership, end-to-end GTM or pricing ownership, direct hiring/performance authority, or advanced experimentation-first AI leadership; advanced experimentation-heavy and advanced academic signals are 0/31 (0.0%).
citations: Soft skills: communication 30/31 (96.8%), collaboration 27/31 (87.1%), stakeholder management 21/31 (67.7%), ownership 16/31 (51.6%).
Deep analysis: delivery 17/17, scale 17/17, evaluation 15/17, architecture 11/17.
Management leadership: hiring 8/31 (25.8%), performance management 8/31 (25.8%), org building 12/31 (38.7%), budget/P&L 2/31 (6.5%).

Pain points: define and prioritize AI product roadmaps 6 mentions; prototype, test, and deploy AI product capabilities 3; align priorities with engineering, design, leadership, and executives 3; drive adoption and end-to-end product outcomes 3.

themes.theme: AI platform and GenAI capability delivery
themes.why_it_matters: This category rewards shipped AI outcomes over abstract AI interest; delivery and scale are valued in 17/17 deep analyses each, and pain points center on prototyping, testing, deploying, and launching AI capabilities.
themes.ats_guidance: Use as a strongest keyword if true: repeat "AI platform", "GenAI", or equivalent 2-3 times across headline, profile, one core experience bullet, and skills.
themes.citation: Deep analysis valued evidence: delivery 17/17, scale 17/17; pain points: prototype/test/deploy AI capabilities 3 mentions, AI product launches from concept to launch 3.

themes.why_it_matters: Architecture proof is a major separator because platform design appears in 17/31 roles (54.8%) and architecture is cited in 11/17 deep analyses.
themes.ats_guidance: Use as a strongest keyword if true: repeat "platform design", "architecture", or equivalent 2-3 times across headline/profile/experience; prefer outcome-linked mentions over tool lists.
themes.citation: Architecture: platform_design_pct 17/31 (54.8%); valued evidence type architecture 11/17.

themes.why_it_matters: The category favors leaders who can build new AI capabilities and harden them for scale, not just maintain mature systems.
themes.ats_guidance: Use as a secondary keyword if true: mention "0->1", "greenfield", or "productionization" 1-2 times in experience bullets only.
themes.citation: Greenfield split: both 19 roles, greenfield 4, optimization 1.

themes.why_it_matters: Evaluation is not universal in the postings, but it is one of the clearest top-third signals in deep analysis and maps directly to shipping trusted AI systems.
themes.ats_guidance: Use as a strongest keyword if true: repeat "evaluation", "quality", or equivalent 2-3 times across profile, experience, and skills; pair each mention with a metric.
themes.citation: AI/ML stack: evaluation_quality 7/31 (22.6%); valued evidence type evaluation 15/17; success metrics quality 10/17.

themes.theme: Guardrails, governance, and privacy-aware AI delivery
themes.why_it_matters: EEA-flavored roles reward trust and compliance signals when they are tied to product delivery, especially governance, guardrails, and privacy handling.
themes.ats_guidance: Use as a secondary keyword if true: mention "guardrails", "governance", "GDPR", or equivalent 1-2 times across profile and one experience bullet; do not claim formal compliance ownership unless true.
themes.citation: AI/ML stack: guardrails_governance 6/31 (19.4%); governance valued evidence type 4/17; GDPR 10/31 (32.3%).

themes.why_it_matters: Reliability is a useful supporting proof point for manager-level AI platform roles, but it is not a universal category driver.
themes.ats_guidance: Use as a niche keyword if true: mention reliability, routing, observability, or equivalent once in a high-signal bullet only.
themes.citation: Valued evidence types: reliability 4/17; AI/ML observability LangSmith 2/31 (6.5%); model serving/routing 1/31 (3.2%).

themes.why_it_matters: This category consistently expects the manager to align technical execution with broader priorities across engineering, design, leadership, and executives.
themes.ats_guidance: Use as a strongest keyword if true: repeat "stakeholder management" or equivalent 2-3 times across profile and experience; keep it concrete with decisions, tradeoffs, or roadmap outcomes.
themes.citation: Soft skills: stakeholder management 21/31 (67.7%), communication 30/31 (96.8%), collaboration 27/31 (87.1%); pain point: align priorities with engineering, design, leadership, and executives 3 mentions.

themes.theme: Roadmap and prioritization contribution without PM inflation
themes.why_it_matters: Roadmap definition appears in the pain points, but deep analysis explicitly warns against overstating formal product-management ownership.
themes.ats_guidance: Use as a secondary keyword if true: mention prioritization, roadmap input, or product partnership 1-2 times, preferably with verbs like "partnered", "translated", or "prioritized" rather than "owned".
themes.citation: Pain point: define and prioritize AI product roadmaps 6 mentions; unsafe claims: do not claim formal product-management ownership unless directly evidenced appears in 3/17 deep analyses.

themes.why_it_matters: Ownership is common, but ambiguity-tolerance is supporting context rather than a primary selector compared with shipped delivery proof.
themes.ats_guidance: Use as a secondary keyword if true: mention ownership 1-2 times in profile or experience; avoid generic soft-skill clusters without quantified outcomes.
themes.citation: Soft skills: ownership 16/31 (51.6%), thriving in ambiguity 9/31 (29.0%), entrepreneurial mindset 10/31 (32.3%).

themes.theme: Org building and mentoring when explicitly evidenced
themes.why_it_matters: People leadership exists in the category, but it is not universal enough to anchor the CV unless there is direct evidence.
themes.ats_guidance: Use as a secondary keyword if true: mention team growth, mentoring, or org design 1-2 times; include team size or direct reports if available.
themes.citation: Management leadership: org building 12/31 (38.7%), hiring 8/31 (25.8%), performance management 8/31 (25.8%); soft skill mentoring 9/31 (29.0%); direct report samples include 4, 6-8, and about 10.

themes.why_it_matters: Remote and distributed collaboration is common enough to support the story, especially for EEA hiring, but it should stay subordinate to delivery evidence.
themes.ats_guidance: Use as a niche keyword if true: mention remote-first or distributed once in the summary or a role header, not repeatedly.
themes.citation: Market context collaboration model: remote_first 17/31 (54.8%), distributed 3/31 (9.7%), hybrid 2/31 (6.5%).

themes.theme: Business value, adoption, and user impact measurement
themes.why_it_matters: Top-third profiles show business-facing evidence of shipped value, not only technical implementation detail.
themes.ats_guidance: Use as a strongest keyword cluster if true: repeat adoption, user impact, or measurable value 2-3 times across profile and experience, always with numbers.
themes.citation: Success metrics: measurable value 10/17, quality 10/17, delivery 10/17, user impact 10/17, adoption 6/17.

key_achievement_archetypes.archetype: Shipped AI platform or workflow capability with measurable adoption
key_achievement_archetypes.what_it_proves: Proves the core category fit: delivery, scale, user value, and end-to-end execution of AI capabilities.
pain_points_addressed: prototype, test, and deploy ai product capabilities
drive adoption and end-to-end product outcomes
driving ai product launches from concept to launch
metrics_to_include: time to launch or time to value
workflow volume, plugin count, or integration count
revenue influence, productivity gain, or cost avoided
key_achievement_archetypes.story_format_guidance: Use STAR or ARIS with the product or platform problem first, the shipped AI capability second, and measurable adoption or value third. Keep the action centered on delivery and iteration rather than abstract strategy.
key_achievement_archetypes.citation: Valued evidence: delivery 17/17, scale 17/17; success metrics measurable value 10/17, delivery 10/17, user impact 10/17, adoption 6/17.

key_achievement_archetypes.archetype: Designed platform architecture that made AI delivery production-ready
key_achievement_archetypes.what_it_proves: Proves the candidate can move beyond demos into stable AI systems through architecture, integration, and productionization.
pain_points_addressed: prototype, test, and deploy ai product capabilities
align priorities with engineering, design, leadership, and executives
metrics_to_include: cost per request or infrastructure efficiency
key_achievement_archetypes.story_format_guidance: Frame the story around a scaling bottleneck or integration challenge, then show the architectural choice, rollout, and production outcome. Use concrete system nouns, not generic innovation language.
key_achievement_archetypes.citation: Architecture: platform design 17/31 (54.8%); valued evidence type architecture 11/17; greenfield split both 19 and greenfield 4.

key_achievement_archetypes.archetype: Improved AI quality, trust, and governance so the system could scale safely
key_achievement_archetypes.what_it_proves: Proves the candidate can ship AI without breaking trust by adding evaluation, guardrails, privacy, or governance mechanisms.
pain_points_addressed: prototype, test, and deploy ai product capabilities
drive adoption and end-to-end product outcomes
metrics_to_include: policy coverage or governance checkpoint adoption
privacy or compliance audit pass rate
user satisfaction or trust signal improvement
key_achievement_archetypes.story_format_guidance: Describe the trust risk clearly, then show the evaluation or governance mechanism you introduced and the measurable quality outcome. Keep compliance language delivery-linked, not legalistic.
key_achievement_archetypes.citation: AI/ML stack: evaluation_quality 7/31 (22.6%), guardrails_governance 6/31 (19.4%); governance valued evidence type 4/17; GDPR 10/31 (32.3%).

key_achievement_archetypes.archetype: Aligned cross-functional stakeholders around AI priorities and converted that into shipped outcomes
key_achievement_archetypes.what_it_proves: Proves business-to-technical translation, prioritization judgment, and delivery leadership without requiring inflated PM ownership.
pain_points_addressed: define and prioritize ai product roadmaps
align priorities with engineering, design, leadership, and executives
turn data and user feedback into actionable product decisions
metrics_to_include: stakeholder adoption or usage by team
number of teams aligned or enabled
key_achievement_archetypes.story_format_guidance: Use verbs such as partnered, translated, prioritized, and aligned unless formal PM ownership is explicit. The result sentence should show what shipped or improved because of that alignment.
key_achievement_archetypes.citation: Soft skills: communication 30/31 (96.8%), collaboration 27/31 (87.1%), stakeholder management 21/31 (67.7%); pain points roadmap 6 mentions, alignment 3, user-feedback-to-decisions 3.

key_achievement_archetypes.archetype: Built or shaped a small AI engineering team or operating model
key_achievement_archetypes.what_it_proves: Proves management maturity when explicit, especially org design, mentoring, or team operating cadence.
pain_points_addressed: define and prioritize ai product roadmaps
drive adoption and end-to-end product outcomes
key_achievement_archetypes.story_format_guidance: Only use this archetype if the evidence is direct. Lead with team scope, then show how the team shape improved delivery or quality; do not drift into generic people-leadership language.
key_achievement_archetypes.citation: Management leadership: org building 12/31 (38.7%), hiring 8/31 (25.8%), performance management 8/31 (25.8%); direct report samples: 4, 6-8, about 10.

highest_weight_roles: AI platform, GenAI platform, or AI workflow roles with shipped capabilities and measurable adoption.
Engineering-manager or tech-lead roles that combine architecture proof with cross-functional stakeholder translation.
Player-coach roles that show evaluation, governance, or reliability work tied to production AI delivery.
expand_in_work_history: Roles with direct AI delivery, scale, architecture, or evaluation evidence because delivery and scale appear in 17/17 deep analyses and architecture in 11/17.
Roles with measurable adoption, user impact, or quality outcomes because those success metrics each appear in 10/17 deep analyses.
Roles showing roadmap contribution and stakeholder alignment if the bullets clearly show partnered execution rather than inflated PM ownership.
compress_in_work_history: Older backend or distributed-systems work with no AI, adoption, or stakeholder outcome; keep it to execution credibility rather than the headline story.
Roles dominated by generic management language without evidence of shipped AI capabilities, architecture, or measurable value.
Tool-heavy or marketing-heavy bullets that read like PM operations unless those tools and responsibilities are truly central to the role.
how_to_frame_non_ai_experience: Use non-AI backend, platform, or microservices work as proof of execution depth, systems thinking, and credibility with engineering teams, then bridge it to later AI delivery.
Show how earlier distributed-systems work enabled later AI productionization, reliability, or integration work rather than leaving it as a separate non-AI identity.
Do not let non-AI experience outvote the AI delivery narrative when stronger AI platform evidence exists.
citations: Deep analysis valued evidence: delivery 17/17, scale 17/17, evaluation 15/17, architecture 11/17.
Success metrics: measurable value 10/17, quality 10/17, delivery 10/17, user impact 10/17.
Candidate-mapping theme frequency indicates prior backend work is weaker than AI workflow/platform evidence for this category: similar guidance appears in 3/17 deep analyses.
Role scope favors manager and player-coach over executive: manager 14/31 (45.2%), player-coach 5/31 (16.1%), executive 1/31 (3.2%).
Soft-skill emphasis should stay operational because communication 30/31 (96.8%), collaboration 27/31 (87.1%), and stakeholder management 21/31 (67.7%) are expected.
Research-heavy and PhD signals are both 0/31 (0.0%), and prompt engineering is 0/31 (0.0%), so language should stay delivery-first rather than hype- or research-first.

avoid_claims: Do not claim formal product-management ownership of roadmaps, pricing, GTM, or product marketing unless directly evidenced; similar warnings recur in 2-3/17 deep analyses.

Do not claim people management, hiring, performance management, or budget ownership unless explicit; hiring and performance management each appear in only 8/31 roles (25.8%), and budget/P&L in 2/31 (6.5%).
Do not claim hands-on use of Airtable, Asana, HubSpot, Notion, Canva, or similar tools unless actually used; each appears in 12-13/31 roles because the title set is noisy and PM-leaning.
Do not imply research, publications, or PhD-level AI work; research-heavy and PhD signals are 0/31 (0.0%).
buzzword_patterns: Avoid generic 'AI strategy' language with no shipped outcome; this category values delivery and scale in 17/17 deep analyses each.
Avoid broad 'agentic AI' or 'prompt engineering' branding without evidence; agents/orchestration appears in 8/31 (25.8%) but prompt engineering appears in 0/31 (0.0%).
Avoid long tool lists from PM/ops software unless those tools are actually central to the candidate's work; the top hard-skill cluster peaks at only 13/31 (41.9%) and is not universal.
title_inflation_risks: Do not swap an engineering-leadership role into Product Manager, AI or AI Product Manager without direct PM evidence, even though Product Manager, AI appears in 13/31 titles.
Do not inflate to Head of AI, VP, or executive ownership when executive scope appears in only 1/31 roles (3.2%).
Use Senior AI Engineering Manager only when the work history shows senior-manager-level scope, org-building, or broader remit, not just strong IC delivery.
research_framing_risks: Do not add publications, research agenda, or academic framing to strengthen the CV; the category is explicitly not research-heavy at 0/31 (0.0%).
Do not over-index on ML frameworks such as PyTorch, TensorFlow, or Hugging Face unless they were genuinely used; each appears in only 1/31 roles (3.2%).
domain_or_region_risks: Do not imply deep GDPR or regional compliance ownership from EEA targeting alone; mention GDPR once only if directly evidenced, even though it appears in 10/31 roles (32.3%).
Do not assume native-language requirements; none appear in the 17 deep analyses.
Do not overfit the CV around enterprise SaaS or consulting language unless the background matches; domain signals are mixed, with enterprise SaaS at 4 and consulting at 1.
citations: Unsafe claims top patterns recur across deep analyses: formal PM ownership 3/17, pricing or GTM ownership 3/17, tool-use inflation 3/17, people management or budget ownership 2-3/17.
Management leadership: hiring 8/31 (25.8%), performance management 8/31 (25.8%), org building 12/31 (38.7%), budget/P&L 2/31 (6.5%).
Hard-skill noise cluster: Airtable 13/31 (41.9%), Asana 13/31 (41.9%), HubSpot 13/31 (41.9%), Notion 13/31 (41.9%), Canva 12/31 (38.7%).
Research-heavy 0/31 (0.0%), requires_phd 0/31 (0.0%), prompt engineering 0/31 (0.0%), frameworks each 1/31 (3.2%).

overall_assessment.one_sentence_verdict: Candidate has strong curated evidence for AI platform delivery, architecture, evaluation, governance, and player-coach leadership that aligns well with the category's delivery-led manager archetype, though the representation layer needs reframing from 'Technical Architect' toward 'AI Engineering Manager' language and better surfacing of adoption metrics and stakeholder translation proof.
overall_assessment.uncertainty_note: Representation assessment is currently in proxy mode because no canonical master CV artifact was found. Formal management title is not explicit in evidence—player-coach framing is accurate and safe for this category where manager scope appears in 45.2% of roles and player-coach in 16.1%, but avoid claiming direct report counts or formal performance management unless explicitly evidenced.
citations: Blueprint role scope: manager 14/31 (45.2%), player-coach 5/31 (16.1%), executive 1/31 (3.2%)
Commander-4 platform lead with 2,000 users, 42 plugins — data/master-cv/roles/01_seven_one_entertainment.md:28
Mentored 10+ senior engineers, promoted 3 to leads — data/master-cv/roles/01_seven_one_entertainment.md:231

weighted_score_explanation: Evidence coverage (8.5 × 30% = 2.55): Commander-4 delivers strong AI platform proof—hybrid retrieval, evaluation harnesses, guardrails, semantic caching, and MCP tools all map directly to category table-stakes and differentiators.

Curation completeness (7.5 × 15% = 1.125): Most key evidence is already curated; upstream STAR records could strengthen stakeholder and technical vision narratives but core AI platform work is in place.

Representation quality (7.0 × 25% = 1.75): Current headline 'Technical Architect / AI Platform Architect' is defensible but not optimized; player-coach and AI platform language appears in summary but adoption metrics and stakeholder translation could be foregrounded.

AI architecture fit (9.0 × 15% = 1.35): Excellent platform design evidence—hybrid search, semantic caching, guardrails, evaluation harness, MCP integrations, structured outputs—matches the category's 54.8% platform design signal.

Leadership scope fit (7.0 × 10% = 0.70): Mentoring 10+ engineers, promoting 3 to leads, and hiring evidence supports player-coach but formal manager title is not explicit; safe for category where 45.2% are manager scope.

Impact proof strength (7.5 × 5% = 0.375): Quantified outcomes exist (2,000 users, €30M GDPR protection, 75% incident reduction) but AI-specific adoption and user impact metrics could be more explicit.
citations: Platform design 17/31 (54.8%) — blueprint category_signature
Delivery 17/17, scale 17/17, evaluation 15/17 — blueprint evidence_ledger

gap_analysis_top.signal: Measurable AI adoption and user impact metrics
gap_analysis_top.why_it_matters: Success metrics measurable value, user impact, and adoption appear in 10/17, 10/17, and 6/17 deep analyses respectively; top-third profiles show business-facing evidence.
gap_analysis_top.current_state: Commander-4 evidence states 2,000 users and 42 plugins but does not foreground adoption growth percentage, user retention, or workflow efficiency gains explicitly.
gap_analysis_top.safe_interpretation: User count and plugin count are factual; can safely claim platform adoption at scale but should not invent percentages without evidence.
gap_analysis_top.recommended_action: Surface adoption metrics more prominently in summary and role bullets if additional quantified evidence exists; otherwise keep current user count as primary proof.
gap_analysis_top.citation: Blueprint success metrics: measurable value 10/17, user impact 10/17, adoption 6/17

gap_analysis_top.why_it_matters: Blueprint recommends safe title families 'AI Engineering Manager' or 'Engineering Manager, AI Platform'; current headline 'Technical Architect / AI Platform Architect' indexes on IC architecture rather than player-coach leadership.
gap_analysis_top.current_state: Headline in data/master-cv/role_metadata.json uses architect title; summary mentions player-coach but headline does not signal management scope.
gap_analysis_top.safe_interpretation: Player-coach framing is safe given mentoring 10+ engineers and promoting 3 to leads; avoid claiming 'Engineering Manager' as formal title unless the role actually carries that title.
gap_analysis_top.recommended_action: Consider headline variant 'Engineering Leader / AI Platform Architect' or 'Technical Lead, AI Platform' to bridge architecture strength with leadership signal without overclaiming formal manager title.
gap_analysis_top.citation: Blueprint headline_pattern safe_title_families: AI Engineering Manager, Engineering Manager AI Platform

gap_analysis_top.why_it_matters: Pain point 'define and prioritize AI product roadmaps' has 6 mentions in blueprint; differentiator if shown as partnered execution rather than PM ownership.
gap_analysis_top.current_state: Evidence shows stakeholder collaboration and technical debt ROI communication but does not explicitly name roadmap contribution or prioritization decisions.
gap_analysis_top.safe_interpretation: Can safely reference roadmap translation and prioritization input if framed as engineering perspective, not product ownership.
gap_analysis_top.recommended_action: If candidate has evidence of informing or shaping AI roadmap decisions, curate that evidence; otherwise, frame current stakeholder collaboration as roadmap translation.
gap_analysis_top.citation: Blueprint pain points: define and prioritize AI product roadmaps 6 mentions

gap_analysis_top.why_it_matters: Greenfield split shows 'both' in 19 roles and 'greenfield' in 4; category favors leaders who can build new AI capabilities and harden for scale.
gap_analysis_top.current_state: Commander-4 evidence implies greenfield platform work but does not explicitly frame the 0→1 launch journey; Daypaio event-sourced CRM shows 0→1 but is non-AI.
gap_analysis_top.safe_interpretation: Commander-4 can be framed as greenfield AI platform delivery if the work started from scratch; avoid implying 0→1 if it was an enhancement to existing platform.
gap_analysis_top.recommended_action: Clarify Commander-4 origin story—if greenfield, make that explicit in role bullets; otherwise frame as platform extension and scaling.

gap_analysis_top.current_state: Curated evidence at 01_seven_one_entertainment.md:266 states 'Led hiring process for software engineers' but does not quantify hires or hiring authority.
gap_analysis_top.safe_interpretation: Can safely claim hiring process leadership; avoid implying direct hiring authority or headcount ownership without explicit evidence.
gap_analysis_top.recommended_action: If upstream STAR records contain quantified hiring outcomes (e.g., hired X engineers, built team from Y to Z), curate that detail; otherwise keep current framing.

gap_analysis_top.current_state: No explicit evidence of conducting performance reviews, setting compensation, or formal 1:1 management cadence; mentoring and promotion support are evidenced but not formal PM.
gap_analysis_top.safe_interpretation: Player-coach mentoring and career development support are safe; formal performance management claims would be unsafe without explicit evidence.
